headline: Maine's Silver Torrent Returns: River Herring Make Historic Comeback
meta_description: After centuries of decline, Maine's alewife population has surged to 20 million fish thanks to dam removal efforts and fishing protections.
summary_short: Once nearly extinct in Maine's rivers, the alewife has made a stunning recovery. In 2025, 20 million river herring spawned upstream—the largest run in decades.
body:
Imagine a quiet brook suddenly transformed into a torrent of silver. That's what happens each spring when river herring, or alewives, migrate upstream to spawn across Maine—a sight that once seemed lost to history.

For centuries, these small fish sustained everyone from Native Americans to colonial settlers. But beginning in 1840, dam construction began strangling the rivers. By 1994, annual catches had plummeted from an average of 3 million pounds to just 150,000 pounds. Offshore commercial fishing had decimated populations in undammed rivers too.

Then came restoration. Starting in 1999, a coordinated effort to remove dams from Maine's river systems began. When a fishing moratorium on alewives was declared in 2012, the turning point arrived. The removal of two major dams on the Penobscot River proved transformative. Additional fish channels were constructed around remaining obstacles.

By 2025, the results were undeniable: 20 million alewives ran up Maine's rivers, the highest level in decades. "We've gone from losing the fish completely in a lot of river systems due to damming and pollution, and now we have some of the largest populations in the globe," said Rustin Taylor, executive director of the Alewife Harvesters of Maine.

The Passamaquoddy tribe calls them siqonomeq—"the fish that feeds all." After centuries of decline, Maine's waters prove that recovery, though long, remains possible.
